subjects.subject: film-photography
subjects.title: Caring for Cameras, Lenses, and Film
subjects.lesson:
First principles: what harms your gear
Cameras and film are simple machines exposed to complex environments. Their enemies are moisture (fungus, corrosion), heat (warping, fogging, film aging), dust and grit (scratches), skin oils and solvents (coating damage), and time (degrading seals and lubricants). If you control moisture and temperature, keep optics clean with gentle methods, and replace perishable parts before they fail, most problems never appear.

Cleaning optics and viewfinders
- Touch last, not first. Dust usually sits loose on the surface; wiping it in circles can turn dust into sandpaper. Start with a hand-squeezed blower to remove loose particles. Avoid canned air at close range; propellant can spit and cold-shock glass.
- Use a soft brush next. A clean, dedicated lens brush or a dry, high-quality microfiber can lift remaining specks. Keep the brush capped so it doesn’t collect grit in your bag.
- Only then, use liquid—sparingly. Apply a drop of lens cleaning solution (or a 50/50 mix of distilled water and isopropyl 99%) to a lens tissue or microfiber, never directly on the glass. Gently wipe in straight lines from center outward, replacing the tissue area as it picks up oils. Do not rub a stubborn spot; soak it with a bit more solution and let chemistry do the work.
- Viewfinders and focusing screens scratch easily. Remove dust with a blower only. If a screen is filthy, it’s safer to have a technician clean it. Rangefinder windows can be treated like lenses, but be extra gentle.
- Filters as sacrificial layers. A clean, multi-coated UV or clear filter protects the front element from scratches and fingerprints. Keep the filter clean, too; a dirty filter degrades contrast just like a dirty lens.

Bodies, shutters, and batteries
- Exterior care. Wipe the body with a slightly damp microfiber; avoid household cleaners. Leatherette lifts with harsh solvents.
- Shutter exercise. Focal-plane shutters and aperture mechanisms benefit from occasional cycling. Fire through a range of speeds monthly to keep lubricants distributed.
- Battery compartments. Corrosion shows up as white or blue crust. Remove batteries when storing for more than a few weeks. If you see corrosion, lightly swab with isopropyl on a cotton bud; let it dry fully. Severe corrosion needs professional attention.

Film care and storage
- Film is perishable chemistry. Heat speeds fogging and color shift; cold slows it. For unshot film, aim for cool, stable storage. Refrigeration is ideal for months; freezing is best for long-term. Keep boxes sealed to prevent moisture absorption.
- Warm up before opening. When removing film from the fridge or freezer, let it reach room temperature inside its sealed bag (refrigerated: ~1 hour; frozen: several hours). This prevents condensation on the emulsion.
- After exposure, process soon. Latent images slowly fade and fog. If you must delay, store exposed rolls cool and dry, in sealed bags with desiccant.
- Light and X-rays. Keep film in opaque canisters. Carry-on airport scanners are generally safe up to moderate ISOs, but repeated scans add up; ask for hand-check for high-speed or pushed film. Avoid checked luggage; those scanners are stronger.

Preventing fungus, corrosion, and light leaks
- Fungus needs moisture, darkness, and time. Keep relative humidity around 35–50%. In humid climates, use a dry cabinet or a sealed container with rechargeable silica gel packs. Recharge desiccant when its indicator changes color.
- Airflow beats sealed damp. A sealed box trapping humid air invites fungus. Either use a true dry cabinet or ensure desiccant is present and maintained.
- Sunlight as a mild deterrent. Occasional indirect sunlight on a lens (caps off, not heating it) can discourage spores but won’t cure established growth. Never bake a lens on a windowsill.
- Corrosion control. Wipe metal contact points occasionally with a dry cloth. Don’t apply oils inside the camera. If you shoot at the beach, wipe salt mist from exterior surfaces with a slightly damp cloth, then dry thoroughly.
- Light seals. Foam door and mirror bumper seals degrade into goo or dust. Symptoms: fogged edges, frame leaks, sticky doors. Replace seals proactively using pre-cut kits or have a technician do it.

Storage environment and habits
- Temperature: aim for stable, moderate temps (15–21°C / 59–70°F). Avoid attic/garage extremes.
- Humidity: 35–50% RH. Too dry can shrink some materials; too humid invites fungus. Measure with a small hygrometer.
- Containers: breathable shelves in dry rooms are fine in temperate climates. In humid areas, prefer dry cabinets. If using cases, open them occasionally to air out.
- Bags: never store long-term in padded camera bags; foams can off-gas and trap moisture. Bags are for transport.
- Label and rotate film: write purchase dates and ISOs; use older stock first. Keep a small notebook or phone note of what is refrigerated or loaded.

Simple routines that extend life
- After a shoot, brush and blow the camera, cap lenses, and let gear acclimate to room conditions before shelving.
- Once a month: exercise shutters, check batteries, glance at seals and lens elements for haze.
- Each season: refresh desiccant, audit film stash, and wipe exterior surfaces. Address any stickiness or residue—small problems grow when ignored.

The big idea: gentle, dry, and regular. Clean with the least force possible, keep moisture in check, and make inspection a habit. Your reward is consistency: sharp images, reliable mechanics, and film that develops as expected.
subjects.example:
Quick, safe front-element clean:
1) Blow away loose dust with a hand blower, holding the lens face-down so gravity helps.
2) Sweep the surface with a capped-when-not-in-use lens brush.
3) Fold a lens tissue, add one drop of lens cleaner to the tissue (not the glass).
4) Wipe in straight lines from center outward, rotating to a fresh part of the tissue as it picks up oil.
5) Finish with a dry, clean section to lift any remaining moisture. Inspect under soft light. Stop if a mark resists; re-soak and lift rather than scrubbing.
subjects.scenario: You move to a coastal, humid city. You buy a small electronic dry cabinet and set it to 45% RH. Lenses and bodies live there uncapped (but with rear caps) so air circulates through. Each shelf has a labeled tray: bodies, primes, zooms. Your film sits in the fridge door in a sealed box with two silica gel packets and a note: “Warm 1 hr before opening.” After beach shoots, you wipe bodies with a slightly damp cloth, dry them, then let them rest outside the cabinet for 30 minutes to avoid trapping moisture before returning them. Once a month you cycle your shutters, recharge desiccant, and check light seals—ordering a replacement kit when the mirror bumper feels tacky. Six months later, no fungus, no leaks, and your negatives are clean and consistent.
subjects.reflection_question: If you had to choose only one environmental control for your gear—humidity, temperature, or airflow—which would you prioritize in your climate, and what trade-offs would that create for the other two?

subjects.subject: microeconomics
subjects.title: Income and Substitution Effects—Intuition
subjects.lesson:
Two forces behind every price change
When a price changes, your choice moves for two reasons at once. First, the relative price changes: the good that got cheaper now buys more compared to other things. That pushes you to substitute toward it. Second, your purchasing power changes: if something you buy got cheaper, your budget stretches; if it got pricier, your budget shrinks. That makes you act as if your income changed. These are the substitution and income effects.

Substitution effect: hold satisfaction steady
Imagine you want to keep the same level of satisfaction while prices shift. If coffee gets cheaper relative to tea, keeping the same satisfaction, you can swap some tea for coffee and do at least as well. The substitution effect always moves you toward the relatively cheaper option and away from the relatively more expensive one. It’s about reshuffling within the same overall happiness, exploiting a better deal.

Income effect: purchasing power shifts
Now allow your satisfaction to change with your new effective income. If coffee gets cheaper and you buy coffee at all, your budget buys more total satisfaction than before. For normal goods, more purchasing power leads you to want more of them. For inferior goods, more purchasing power leads you to want less of them. If the price rises, the signs flip: you feel poorer; you cut back on normal goods and might buy more of some inferior staples.

Normal, inferior, and Giffen goods
- Normal good: when effective income rises, demand rises. Most goods behave this way over typical ranges (fresh vegetables, dining out, quality coffee).
- Inferior good: when effective income rises, demand falls. These are goods you rely on when money is tight but trade away from when you can (instant noodles, bus rides for someone who upgrades to ride-hailing).
- Giffen good: a rare case where a good is so inferior and takes such a large share of the budget that a price drop makes you buy less of it. Why? The negative income effect (feeling richer leads you to shift away from the inferior staple toward better foods) overwhelms the positive substitution effect (it got cheaper). Classic examples involve staple foods in severe poverty.

How to predict the direction qualitatively
- Start with substitution: it’s reliable and always points toward the relatively cheaper good. That’s your baseline nudge.
- Weigh the income effect by budget share: the bigger the share of spending, the stronger the income effect. A small-price change in a tiny category barely changes your effective income.
- Decide normal vs inferior: ask, “If I felt a bit richer, would I want more or less of this?” That answers the sign of the income effect.
- Combine them: if both effects point the same way, demand changes a lot. If they oppose, the net result depends on which is stronger.

Hicksian vs Marshallian, in plain language
The Hicksian (compensated) view imagines adjusting your income to keep your satisfaction level unchanged while prices move. That isolates the pure substitution reshuffle. The Marshallian (uncompensated) view looks at your actual outcome with your real budget after the price change. The gap between them is the income effect. You don’t need equations to use this: first think “same happiness, better deal” (substitution), then think “now I’m a bit richer or poorer” (income).

Heuristics you can use
- Close substitutes mean strong substitution effects. If tea and coffee are close in your mind, a small price change can cause a big switch.
- Necessities with few substitutes and large budget shares mean strong income effects. Rent hikes squeeze everything else.
- Inferior goods show up when you imagine upgrading. If you picture escaping a thing when you get a raise, it’s inferior to you.

Pitfalls
- Don’t label whole categories. A bus ride can be normal for one person (who values it for convenience) and inferior for another (who would prefer to drive if richer). These are personal and context-specific.
- Beware extremes. Giffen behavior requires a narrow, harsh context: a staple dominating the budget and limited access to substitutes.

Putting it together
When a price falls, ask: 1) What do I switch into because it’s relatively cheaper? 2) With the money freed up, what do I now want more or less of? Your qualitative predictions come from those two answers. Over many goods, these small forces add up to explain demand curves sloping downward most of the time—and the interesting exceptions when they don’t.
subjects.example: Streaming vs movie tickets: Suppose your local theater drops ticket prices by 25%. Substitution effect: the theater experience is now relatively cheaper than a home streaming rental, so you plan more theater nights and fewer rentals. Income effect: because your entertainment basket costs less overall, you feel a bit richer; if movie outings are a normal good for you, you add an extra outing this month beyond the substitution shift. Net: more theater, fewer rentals. If, however, theater outings are inferior to you (maybe you dislike crowds and would upgrade to premium home viewing when richer), the income effect could partially offset your substitution toward theaters.
subjects.scenario: Your staple rice price rises by 10%. Substitution effect: rice is now relatively more expensive than pasta or potatoes; you plan to swap in more of those. Income effect: your food budget buys less overall, so you feel poorer. If rice is a normal good for you, you reduce rice even more because feeling poorer makes you cut back. If rice is inferior (you’d prefer quinoa and fresh bread when you can afford it), feeling poorer might push you to eat more rice despite its higher price—offsetting some substitution away. Which dominates depends on how big rice is in your budget and how close the substitutes feel to you.
subjects.reflection_question: Pick one good you buy monthly. If its price fell 20%, what would you substitute toward, and would feeling richer make you want more or less of that same good? Explain which force would dominate for you and why.

subjects.subject: Microsoft 365 Administrator (MS-102)
subjects.title: Network connectivity requirements overview
subjects.lesson:
First principles: SaaS wants the shortest, cleanest path
Microsoft 365 is delivered over the public internet. Performance depends more on path quality than on raw bandwidth. The path of least resistance is local egress to the internet, short DNS resolution to nearby Microsoft entry points, minimal middleboxes, and low round-trip latency. Hairpinning through distant data centers, deep TLS inspection, and chatty proxies add delay and break real-time media.

Endpoint categories you should know
Microsoft groups service endpoints by how sensitive they are to latency and inspection:
- Optimize: small set, performance-critical (Exchange Online front doors, SharePoint/OneDrive front doors, Microsoft Teams media). These should bypass SSL inspection, authentication proxies, and VPN hairpins; allow direct local egress.
- Allow: important but less latency-sensitive; standard proxy processing is acceptable.
- Default: general internet traffic; can follow normal corporate policy.
Maintaining accurate categorization is essential because these lists change. Use Microsoft’s published endpoint web service/feeds to automate updates rather than hardcoding IPs.

Core ports and protocols
- HTTPS/TLS on TCP 443: the backbone for Exchange Online, SharePoint/OneDrive, Teams signaling, and most admin portals.
- HTTP on TCP 80: limited use (CRL/OCSP, redirects). Allow outbound but expect secure redirects.
- Teams media uses UDP primarily (best quality) on 3478–3481 (STUN/TURN) to Microsoft media relays, with additional media flows over UDP 50000–50059 to Microsoft IPs. TCP 443 is a fallback for media if UDP is blocked, but quality suffers. Allow these outbound paths.
- DNS: fast, local resolution is crucial. Use local resolvers that can reach Microsoft authoritative services quickly; avoid forwarding across continents.

Connectivity principles that boost performance
- Local breakout: let Microsoft 365 traffic exit the user’s nearest internet point, not through a central data center. For remote users on VPN, use split tunneling so Optimize endpoints go direct.
- Bypass heavy inspection for Optimize endpoints: deep TLS inspection and auth-challenging proxies increase latency and can break modern TLS features. Create explicit bypass rules for these endpoints.
- Keep hops low: each middlebox (proxy, content filter, IDS) adds delay. Consolidate controls where possible and prefer lightweight allowlisting for Optimize traffic.
- Preserve UDP for Teams: prioritize allowing UDP 3478–3481 and media ports. If blocked, users fall back to TCP and complain about call quality.
- QoS for real-time media: tag Teams audio/video/screen sharing with DSCP and ensure the network honors it end-to-end. Audio typically receives the highest priority.
- Accurate DNS: ensure clients resolve to the nearest Microsoft front door. Avoid forcing DNS through remote resolvers that return suboptimal edges.

Visibility and testing
- Measure the user path: capture latency to Microsoft 365 front doors, packet loss, and jitter. Aim for low double-digit millisecond latency to the nearest edge.
- Use Microsoft’s connectivity test tools to identify suboptimal egress, proxy delays, and geographic mismatches.
- Monitor changes: Microsoft updates endpoints regularly. Subscribe to change feeds and connect them to firewall/proxy automation.

Operating model and change control
- Automate endpoint updates: consume the official web service and generate firewall/proxy changes programmatically. Manual updates drift.
- Separate policy lanes: maintain a distinct rule set for Optimize endpoints so exceptions don’t get buried under generic web rules.
- Document split tunneling: clearly define which routes and FQDNs bypass VPN. Communicate to security and support teams so troubleshooting starts from a shared map.
- Roll out safely: pilot local breakout and UDP enablement with a subset, verify call quality and throughput, then scale.

Security without sacrificing performance
- Principle of least inspection: you can maintain zero trust posture using device compliance, identity, and conditional access while avoiding heavy inline inspection for a small, known set of Optimize endpoints.
- Egress hygiene: still apply egress controls—restrict to Microsoft-owned ranges and FQDNs, log flows, and alert on anomalies. Use cloud-based security for generic web traffic; keep Microsoft 365 off latency-heavy paths.

The big idea
Treat Microsoft 365 like what it is: a latency-sensitive cloud suite. Short, direct, and well-classified paths outperform brute-force bandwidth or one-size-fits-all proxying. Keep endpoint intelligence current, favor local egress with split tunneling, preserve UDP for Teams, and verify with continuous measurement. Better paths yield better user experience and lower support load.
subjects.example:
Practical starter changes for a mid-size office:
- Proxy/PAC: If url matches Optimize endpoints (e.g., outlook.office365.com, sharepoint.com, onedrive.live.com, teams.microsoft.com, and Teams media domains), return DIRECT; otherwise use proxy.
- Firewall egress: Allow TCP 443 to Microsoft 365 Optimize/Allow ranges. Allow UDP 3478–3481 and UDP 50000–50059 outbound to Microsoft media IPs. Log and monitor.
- DNS: Point clients to local resolvers; avoid forwarding to a distant data center.
- VPN: Enable split tunneling for Microsoft 365 Optimize FQDNs and Teams media subnets so clients don’t hairpin through HQ.
subjects.scenario: Your company routes all traffic through a central proxy over MPLS, then to the internet. Teams calls stutter, and OneDrive sync is slow. You pilot local internet breakout at the branch, with PAC rules that send Microsoft 365 Optimize endpoints direct and keep everything else on the proxy. You open UDP 3478–3481 and media ports outbound, and you stop SSL inspection for Optimize FQDNs. Latency to the Microsoft edge drops from 85 ms (hairpinned) to 18 ms (local). Teams MOS scores improve immediately, and OneDrive upload throughput doubles. After a week of clean metrics and satisfied users, you roll the model to remaining branches and update the VPN profile for remote staff with split tunneling.
subjects.reflection_question: Given your current controls, which single change—local egress, proxy bypass for Optimize endpoints, UDP enablement for Teams, or DNS localization—would likely produce the largest user-experience gain with the least security trade-off, and why?

subjects.subject: wisdom
subjects.title: Opportunity Cost
subjects.lesson:
What opportunity cost really is
Every choice silently discards the next-best thing you could have done with the same resources. That forgone alternative is the opportunity cost. It includes time, attention, energy, capital, reputation, and optionality. Cash is obvious; time and focus are the invisible currency most people overspend.

First principles
- Scarcity: you can’t do everything. Saying yes to one path means saying no to others, even if you never say the words out loud.
- Margins matter: evaluate the next hour or next dollar at the margin, not the average. The question is not "Is this good?" but "Is this better than my next-best option right now?"
- Sunk costs are irrelevant: what you’ve already spent can’t be recovered. Only future options matter. Honor lessons learned; ignore money and time already gone.
- Comparative advantage: even if you can do something, you might create more value by doing the thing only you can do and trading for the rest.
- Option value: keeping flexibility has value, especially under uncertainty. A choice that crushes options carries a hidden cost.

Why we miss it
- Salience bias: we see the cost we pay (cash, effort) and miss the ghost cost (what we didn’t do).
- Default momentum: routines become invisible decisions. Repeated meetings, subscriptions, and habits keep collecting opportunity costs quietly.
- Loss aversion: we overprotect what we have and underweight what we could gain by redeploying resources.

A simple decision lens
1) Define the scarce resource at stake (time block, budget, attention).
2) List your top two alternative uses that are realistically on the table.
3) Estimate value in comparable terms (impact, learning, joy, revenue). Be rough but honest.
4) Choose the option with the highest value per unit of the scarce resource.
5) Revisit when facts change. It’s a living decision, not a verdict.

Time as the hardest currency
Money can be borrowed, saved, or replaced; time only moves forward. Protect high-energy hours. If your best creative work happens in the morning, the opportunity cost of a morning meeting is not the meeting—it’s the deep work you didn’t do. A cheap task in an expensive time slot is costly.

Stacking leverage
High-leverage actions produce outsized results for the same input. Templates, automation, reusable processes, training others, and decisions that eliminate whole categories of future decisions all multiply value. The opportunity cost of not building leverage is living on a treadmill of one-off tasks.

Irreversibility and timing
- One-way doors: choices that are expensive to reverse (hiring a key exec, signing a long lease) demand slower, deeper analysis and often a premium on optionality.
- Two-way doors: easily reversible choices (trying a new tool, a small pricing test) should be made quickly, capturing speed’s value. The opportunity cost of overanalyzing two-way doors is time.

Status and signaling traps
Some activities pay in status but not in substance. Ask whether an action’s main payoff is looking busy or looking important. If the true return is signaling, be explicit about that—and compare it to alternatives that build durable value.

Putting it into practice
- Create a stop-doing list for tasks that don’t clear a value threshold.
- Budget attention like money: allocate prime hours to top leverage and defend them.
- Price your time: pick a conservative hourly value for your focused work. Use it to compare build-vs-buy and yes-vs-no choices.
- Track regret signals: if you repeatedly say, “I didn’t have time for X,” that’s an invoice from opportunity cost. Adjust your defaults.

The big idea
Opportunity cost isn’t a theory class; it’s a daily bill. You always pay it, whether you notice or not. The habit to build is noticing what you’re not doing—and choosing on purpose.
subjects.example: Weekend trade-off: You can spend 6 hours designing a simple landing page or pay $300 for a template and light customization. If your focused-hour value is $75, those 6 hours “cost” you $450 in forgone higher-value work or rest. Buying the $300 solution frees 6 hours. If you can use those hours to write sales emails that you expect to generate $1,000, the real price of DIY is not $0—it’s $1,150 ($450 of time + $700 net lost profit). Framed this way, the purchase is cheaper than building.
subjects.scenario: A small nonprofit plans its annual fundraising. Option A: a gala that consumes 300 staff hours, $20,000 in upfront costs, with expected net $60,000 and goodwill signaling to local donors. Option B: a recurring-donor campaign requiring 120 staff hours, $5,000 in costs, with expected net $45,000 this year and higher predictability next year. The gala looks bigger in topline, but the opportunity cost of 180 extra hours is high: those hours could deepen programs or expand grant writing. Weighing money and time together, they pick the recurring campaign and schedule two targeted donor dinners instead of one large event.
subjects.reflection_question: What valuable activity in your life is most often crowded out by lower-value defaults, and what boundary or change would make choosing the higher-value option your new default?

subjects.subject: fiction-writing
subjects.title: Midpoint and Reversal
subjects.lesson:
What the midpoint really does
At the midpoint—roughly the 50% mark—the story pivots. Two things usually happen together: a revelation clarifies the true stakes, and the protagonist shifts from reacting to acting. Think of it as the mirror moment: the character must face an uncomfortable truth about the world, the antagonist, or themselves. Externally, a reversal flips the trajectory: what looked like the plan now looks like a trap, or a seeming loss hides a path to victory.

First principles
- Clarity raises cost: when the character understands what’s really at stake, every later choice costs more. The midpoint makes the price of failure unmistakable.
- Agency increases: before midpoint, the hero often dodges, denies, or improvises. After midpoint, they choose, commit, and pay. The story moves from discovery to pursuit.
- Antagonist pressure tightens: the opposing force stops being abstract. Its reach, method, or motive becomes undeniable, often via a public demonstration.

Forms the midpoint can take
- Revelation: a secret surfaces (the mentor’s betrayal, the real target, the true illness, the hidden map on what looked trivial). This reframes the goal.
- Public price: a humiliation, a loss, a burned bridge that cannot be undone. It narrows choices and forces commitment.
- False victory or false defeat: it looks like they’ve won or lost, but the board has quietly flipped, revealing the larger game.
- Time squeeze: a clock appears or accelerates. Delay now has visible consequences.

Designing your midpoint
1) Name the truth your protagonist has avoided. This is the mirror. What must they admit about themselves to progress?
2) Tie that truth to the external plot. The revelation should change the mission, not just mood.
3) Raise the stakes in two layers: public (what the world can see: risk to others, job, city) and private (identity, love, self-worth). The midpoint should light both fuses.
4) Force a decision. End the scene with a vow, an irreversible step, or an exposed position.
5) Create immediate consequence. Don’t let the vow sit idle; let the world answer back right away.

Stakes Ladder and Mirror Moment
Use the Stakes Ladder to escalate: each rung adds risk or narrows exits. At midpoint, you jump rungs: from inconvenience to consequence, from private to public. The Mirror Moment is the internal articulation: “If I keep lying, I’ll lose her,” or “If I stay small, people get hurt.” Tie the mirror to action in the next beat.

Common pitfalls
- Midpoint as an info dump: revelation without consequence is trivia. If it doesn’t change behavior, it’s not a midpoint.
- Random twist: surprise that isn’t earned feels like a cheat. Seed setups earlier—odd details, strained relationships, small contradictions—so the reveal feels inevitable yet shocking.
- Plateau after the punch: if the story coasts after midpoint, you missed the decision or the consequence. Add a vow and an immediate hit back from the antagonist.

Practical construction template
- Setup: the protagonist pursues a plan rooted in a partial understanding.
- Disruption: a concrete event delivers the truth (document, confession, public exposure, failed attempt that reveals the real barrier).
- Mirror: a beat of recognition ties the truth to the protagonist’s flaw or need.
- Decision: the protagonist chooses a harder, clearer path.
- Cost: an immediate, visible loss signals new stakes and locks the choice.

Scenecraft tips
- Location as crucible: choose a setting that squeezes the protagonist (a boardroom, a hospital hallway, a dinner party) so the decision happens under pressure.
- Opposition present: if possible, put the antagonist or their agent in the room, or make their reach obvious.
- Compress time: fewer cuts, longer beats. Let the audience feel the weight of realizing and choosing.
- Give the protagonist language: a line that crystallizes the vow anchors the second half of the story.

The big idea
Midpoint is not a speed bump; it’s the fulcrum. It turns understanding into commitment and raises the price of every future move. Design it to change the game, not just the page.
subjects.example: Heist midpoint: The crew steals the vault, only to find decoy bonds. Revelation: the client set them up to flush the real courier, who’s leaving tonight. Mirror: the leader admits he took the job to prove he’s still the best, endangering the crew. Decision: he vows to finish the job for them, not his ego, and to intercept the courier. Cost: to act fast, he burns his safe house, exposing the crew’s identities. The second half becomes a chase they chose, with higher stakes and fewer exits.
subjects.scenario: Romantic comedy: Midpoint at a charity gala. The protagonist, pretending to be a philanthropist to land a deal, is asked to make a speech. Revelation: the love interest is the event’s organizer and knows the truth about the protagonist’s shaky finances. Public price: during the speech, a donor publicly questions a fake project; the room turns. Mirror: the protagonist sees that manipulation protects their fear of inadequacy. Decision: they admit, onstage, that they exaggerated and pivot the speech to a genuine appeal for the real cause. Cost: they lose the deal and their boss’s trust. Second half: rebuilding honestly, they pursue the love interest for real, while the ex-boss becomes the antagonist trying to sabotage their new partnership.
subjects.reflection_question: What hard truth has your protagonist refused to face, and how can you force them to confront it at the midpoint in a way that both narrows their choices and deepens their commitment?
